questions.question_text: What right does every co-owner of an immovable have under Louisiana law?
distractors: Veto any sale of the property
Claim exclusive possession of a portion
Receive rent from other co-owners
questions.explanation: Under Civil Code article 807, no one may be compelled to hold a thing in indivision, and any co-owner may demand partition of co-owned property at any time. Co-ownership is considered a temporary arrangement. The one exception: co-owners may agree to exclude partition for a period of up to fifteen years.

questions.question_text: Co-owner A sells her undivided interest in an immovable to a third party without notifying Co-owner B. What is the legal effect?
distractors: The sale is absolutely null
Co-owner B can rescind the sale
The sale requires judicial approval
questions.explanation: Each co-owner has the right to freely alienate, encumber, or lease his undivided interest without the consent of other co-owners. The transfer is valid, and the third party becomes a new co-owner with the same rights.

questions.question_text: Three siblings inherit an immovable in ordinary co-ownership. One sibling dies. Who receives the deceased sibling's share?
distractors: The two surviving siblings equally
The surviving sibling who lives on site
The state through escheat
questions.explanation: Ordinary co-ownership in Louisiana does not include a right of survivorship. When a co-owner dies, his undivided interest passes to his heirs through succession, not to the other co-owners.

questions.question_text: Who may make decisions regarding the use and management of co-owned property?
distractors: Co-owners holding a majority of interests
Any single co-owner acting alone
A court-appointed administrator only
questions.explanation: Under Civil Code article 801, the use and management of the thing held in indivision is determined by agreement of ALL the co-owners — unanimity, not majority rule. If the co-owners cannot agree and partition is not available, a court may determine use and management on petition of a co-owner (article 803). Substantial alterations or improvements likewise require the consent of all co-owners (article 804); a co-owner acting alone may only take necessary steps to preserve the property (article 800).

questions.question_text: Four co-owners each hold 25% of an immovable. Two of them sign a lease of the entire property to a tenant; the other two refuse to consent. What is the effect of the lease?
questions.correct_answer: It is not binding without all co-owners' consent
distractors: It is valid because 50% of the interests signed
It is valid if the rent is shared among all four owners
It is valid for one year and then lapses automatically
questions.explanation: Civil Code article 805 allows a co-owner to freely lease, alienate, or encumber only his own undivided share; the consent of ALL the co-owners is required for the lease, alienation, or encumbrance of the entire thing held in indivision. A lease of the whole signed by co-owners holding only half the interests does not bind the property. When co-owners are deadlocked, a co-owner may petition the court to determine use and management (article 803) or demand partition (article 807).

questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, which asset is classified as community property?
distractors: An immovable inherited by one spouse
A gift donated to one spouse alone
Property owned before the marriage
questions.explanation: Property acquired during the marriage through the effort, skill, or industry of either spouse is community property. Inheritances, donations to one spouse, and property owned before marriage are separate property.

questions.question_text: A husband wants to sell a community immovable. His wife refuses to consent. What is the result?
questions.correct_answer: The husband cannot complete the sale
distractors: The sale proceeds with husband's signature only
The wife's consent is presumed after 10 days
A notary can override the wife's refusal
questions.explanation: Louisiana law requires the consent of both spouses to alienate, encumber, or lease community immovables. Neither spouse acting alone can sell a community immovable without the other's concurrence.

questions.question_text: How is community property divided upon divorce in Louisiana?
distractors: Based on each spouse's income contribution
At the court's discretion per equitable factors
Two-thirds to the primary earner
questions.explanation: Louisiana is a community property state. Upon dissolution of the marriage, community property is divided equally (50/50) between the spouses, regardless of the relative income contributions of each.

questions.question_text: During marriage, a wife receives an immovable as a donation from her parents. Her husband claims it is community property. Who is correct?
questions.correct_answer: The wife, because donations are separate
distractors: The husband, because it was received during marriage
Both, because it becomes community after one year
Neither, until a court classifies the property
questions.explanation: Property acquired by a spouse through donation during the marriage remains that spouse's separate property under Louisiana law, even though it was acquired during the community regime.

questions.question_text: An immovable is purchased during the marriage, and neither spouse offers any proof of the source of the funds. How is the property classified?
distractors: Separate property of each spouse in equal halves
Separate property of the managing spouse
Unclassified until a court renders judgment
questions.explanation: Civil Code article 2340 presumes that things in the possession of a spouse during the community regime are community property. The presumption is rebuttable: either spouse may prove an asset is separate — for example, that it was acquired entirely with separate funds (article 2341). The pre-1980 'double declaration' rule was abolished, so no declaration in the act of acquisition is required to preserve separate status (Tullier v. Tullier, 464 So. 2d 278 (La. 1985)).

questions.question_text: What form is required for a valid matrimonial agreement in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: An authentic act or acknowledged private act
distractors: A private writing without acknowledgment
An oral agreement before two witnesses
A court judgment alone, with no written agreement
questions.explanation: Civil Code article 2331 requires that a matrimonial agreement be made by authentic act (executed before a notary and two witnesses, article 1833) or by an act under private signature duly acknowledged by the spouses. In addition, spouses who wish to modify or terminate their regime during the marriage generally must file a joint petition and obtain a court finding that the change serves their best interests (article 2329); court approval is not required before marriage or during the first year after the spouses move to and acquire a domicile in Louisiana.

questions.question_text: A matrimonial agreement executed during marriage changes the couple's regime from community to separate property. When does this modification take effect toward third parties?
questions.correct_answer: When filed in the parish records
distractors: Immediately upon notarization
Retroactively to the date of marriage
After 90 days from execution
questions.explanation: A matrimonial agreement is effective toward third persons as to immovables only when filed for registry in the conveyance records of the parish where the immovable is situated, and as to movables when filed in the parish or parishes where the spouses are domiciled (Civil Code article 2332). Note that to modify or terminate their regime during marriage the spouses generally must first obtain court approval on joint petition (article 2329).

questions.question_text: Which entity may legally own immovable property in Louisiana?
distractors: Only natural persons may own immovables
Only trusts established by testament
Only Louisiana-domiciled corporations
questions.explanation: LLCs, partnerships, and corporations (whether domestic or qualified foreign entities) may own immovable property in Louisiana. Ownership is not restricted to natural persons.

questions.question_text: In a Louisiana condominium, a unit owner holds title to which interests?
questions.correct_answer: The unit plus undivided common elements
distractors: The individual unit exclusively
A leasehold interest on the unit and land
An undivided share of the entire building
questions.explanation: Louisiana condominium law provides that each unit owner holds exclusive ownership of the individual unit together with an undivided interest in the common elements of the condominium property.

questions.question_text: A member of an LLC plans to sign an act of sale conveying the LLC's immovable. What does Louisiana law provide about the member's authority?
questions.correct_answer: Selling immovables is outside the default mandate
distractors: Any member may always convey LLC immovables alone
A parish court must approve the transfer
The LLC must first convert to a corporation
questions.explanation: Under La. R.S. 12:1317(A), a member or manager is a mandatary of the LLC for all matters in the ordinary course of its business EXCEPT the alienation, lease, or encumbrance of its immovables. Authority to sell an immovable must therefore come from the articles of organization or a specific authorization, and third persons may rely on a certificate of a member or manager establishing that authority (R.S. 12:1317(C)). In practice, the certificate or authorization is recorded with the act of sale so the authority appears in the public records.
